questions.question: Explain Immediate Addressing Mode with an example.
keyPoints: Operand value is directly included in the instruction
Used for constants and initial values
Example: LOAD #100 loads value 100 into register
No memory access needed to fetch operand
Common in arithmetic and initialization operations

questions.question: What is the difference between Direct and Indirect Addressing Modes?
keyPoints: Direct: Address field contains actual operand address
Indirect: Address field contains address of location holding the actual address
Indirect requires two memory accesses
Indirect acts like a pointer/dereference operation
Direct is faster but less flexible

questions.question: List and explain three characteristics of RISC architecture.
keyPoints: Load-Store Architecture: Memory accessed only via LOAD/STORE instructions
Fixed Instruction Length: Typically 32-bit instructions for easy pipelining
Large Register File: Reduces memory access, improves performance
Single-Cycle Execution: Most instructions execute in one clock cycle
Simple Instruction Format: Limited addressing modes, uniform structure

questions.question: What is Indexed Addressing Mode? How is it useful in array processing?
keyPoints: Effective address = Base Address + Index Register Content
Used for accessing array elements sequentially
Index register holds offset from base
Example: Array[i] = Base + (i × Element_Size)
Simplifies loop implementation for array traversal

questions.question: Explain Relative Addressing Mode and its advantages.
keyPoints: Effective address = PC + Displacement (or offset)
Used mainly in branch instructions
Code becomes position-independent (relocatable)
Branch targets calculated relative to current instruction
Enables efficient implementation of loops and conditional branches

questions.question: Compare RISC and CISC architectures on any three parameters.
keyPoints: Instruction Set: RISC = small/simple, CISC = large/complex
Memory Access: RISC = load-store only, CISC = memory-to-memory allowed
Instruction Length: RISC = fixed, CISC = variable
Execution: RISC = single-cycle, CISC = multi-cycle for complex
Implementation: RISC = hardwired, CISC = often microcoded

questions.question: Explain the concept of addressing modes in computer architecture. Describe at least five different addressing modes with suitable examples for each.
keyPoints: Addressing modes define how operands are specified/accessed
Immediate: Operand in instruction itself - LOAD #50
Register: Operand in register - ADD R1,R2
Direct/Absolute: Address in instruction - LOAD 1000
Indirect: Address points to another address - LOAD @1000
Indexed: Base + Index - LOAD 1000(R3)
Relative: PC + offset - used in branches
Base-Register: Base register + displacement
Stack: Top of stack via SP
Each mode has specific use cases and trade-offs

questions.question: Discuss the RISC vs CISC debate in computer architecture. Include the historical context, key differences, advantages, disadvantages, and current state of the art.
keyPoints: CISC emerged in 1960s-70s to reduce program size and assembly coding
RISC emerged in 1980s as counter-approach to simplify processor design
RISC advantages: simpler hardware, faster clock, easier pipelining, better performance/watt
CISC advantages: fewer instructions per program, simpler compiler, code density
Modern reality: Lines blurred - x86 uses RISC-like internals, ARM supports complex extensions
Both benefit from advanced pipelining, caches, and out-of-order execution
Current trend: Design philosophy convergence

questions.question: Explain the Load-Store architecture in RISC processors. Why is it important and what are its advantages?
keyPoints: Only LOAD and STORE instructions can access memory
All arithmetic/logical operations work only on registers
Separates memory access from data processing
Advantages: Simpler instruction set, faster execution per instruction
Allows overlapping memory operations with CPU processing
Enables efficient use of registers as temporary storage
Simplifies instruction pipelining and parallel execution
Reduces memory bandwidth requirements
Makes hardware implementation simpler and more reliable

questions.question: Describe the addressing modes commonly found in RISC architectures and explain why RISC typically supports fewer addressing modes than CISC.
keyPoints: RISC typically supports: Immediate, Register, and PC-relative addressing
Limited modes due to fixed instruction format (32 bits)
Fixed space for addressing bits limits addressing flexibility
RISC philosophy: simpler is better - do more with fewer instruction types
CISC supports many modes: direct, indirect, indexed, base-register, relative, etc.
RISC compensates by using multiple simpler instructions for complex addressing
Simple addressing enables faster decoding and pipelining
Complex addressing handled by software sequences of simple instructions
Trade-off: More instructions needed but faster execution of each
